narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including a solid LSAT score of 39—above the typical 36.7 average—and an undergraduate GPA of 2.7, which is a bit below the usual 3.21 mark. The admissions model, trained on data from countless students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 62.6% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try, tipping the scales toward rejection of your application to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which was the top factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure, as it signals lower performance relative to peers compared to the average decile of about 5.5. Your Year 1 decile of 1 played a strong supporting role in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about early academic struggles well below the typical 5.7 average. On a brighter note, your LSAT score was the biggest force pulling the prediction back toward success, countering some of those academic signals with its above-average strength. Overall, these three elements—your deciles in Years 1 and 3 outweighing the LSAT boost—dominated the model's thinking, unlike many applicants where stronger early grades balance things out more evenly. While full-time status aligns with most students (over 92%), it didn't sway the outcome much here. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, as schools value demonstrated improvement in school performance. You might also seek targeted LSAT prep or bar prediction workshops to build on your testing strength. Reaching out to admissions for feedback or reapplying after addressing these gaps could open doors, especially given affirmative pathways that have supported many Black male applicants like you in turning predictions around. Your LSAT edge shows real potential—focusing there while lifting grades could flip the script next time.
